message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it. Read the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and find the code path / contract / test that makes the PoC impossible or the concern moot. Only conclude survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

Finding M-3: Direct confirm primitives default to presence-only pre-registration gate (authority=None) — bypassable real-verification
File: src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:39-53
Description: _gate_preregistration falls back to require_locked (a pure presence check on locked_at/timestamp_proof) when authority is None, and authority defaults to None on both confirm_unit_level and confirm_whole_corpus. These primitives are study-facing (the adjudication docstring instructs studies to call them inside their confirm callables). A study confirmer that calls confirm_whole_corpus/confirm_unit_level without threading the authority through gets only the sentinel check, which a hand-written timestamp_proof='x' satisfies — i.e. no content-binding, no timestamp attestation, no lock-before-confirm ordering. In the composed run_study pipeline the runner pre-verifies via require_preregistered, so this is defense-in-depth there; but any study (or test/fixture) that drives these primitives directly, or a future runner that forgets to pass authority, silently downgrades pre-registration to a no-op. A safe-by-default API should require the authority, not make real verification opt-in.
PoC: from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind, HypothesisOrigin
from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import confirm_whole_corpus
from dataclasses import replace
h = Hypothesis('h','s',HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS,HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY,'t','r',predicted_direction='greater')
h = replace(h, locked_at='2020-01-01T00:00:00+00:00', timestamp_proof='not-a-real-proof')  # fake lock
# authority omitted -> only require_locked runs -> fake proof accepted
v = confirm_whole_corpus(h, observed=9.0, null_distribution=[0.0,1.0,2.0], alpha=0.1, resample_statistics=[9.0], stability_threshold=0.5)
print(v.label)  # produced a verdict on a hypothesis whose 'lock' was never cryptographically verified

Return survives=false if you can refute it (with the specific reason), survives=true if it withstands a genuine disproof attempt.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, validate the supplied
14	statistics, and map an outcome onto one of the three verdicts honestly (including
15	``indeterminate``).
16	
17	Direction handling (audit pass 1, issue #2): a claim predicts the statistic lies in a
18	particular tail of the null. The empirical p-value is computed against *that* tail, so a
19	claim predicting a low value is confirmable, and a genuine contradiction (significant in the
20	opposite tail) is detectable — both were impossible under the previous direction-blind
21	upper-tail-only implementation.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	import datetime as _dt
27	import math
28	from typing import Iterable, Sequence
29	
30	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
31	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Direction, Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
32	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
33	    TimestampAuthority,
34	    require_preregistered,
35	)
36	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
37	
38	
39	def _gate_preregistration(hypothesis: Hypothesis, authority: TimestampAuthority | None) -> None:
40	    """Pre-registration gate for the confirm primitives.
41	
42	    If ``authority`` is supplied, run the full :func:`require_preregistered` check (content
43	    binding + verifiable timestamp + lock-before-this-moment) — so a study calling a confirm
44	    primitive *directly* (outside a runner) gets the same guarantee the runners enforce. If it
45	    is ``None``, fall back to the cheap presence gate (:func:`require_locked`); the caller has
46	    then opted out of real verification and owns that choice.
47	    """
48	    if authority is not None:
49	        require_preregistered(
50	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
51	        )
52	    else:
53	        require_locked(hypothesis)
54	
55	
56	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
57	    """Cheap **presence** gate: refuse a hypothesis that carries no lock fields at all.
58	
59	    This is defense-in-depth, not the methodology-grade check. It does not verify the proof
60	    binds the content or that the lock precedes confirmation — that is
61	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.require_preregistered`, which the
62	    confirmatory *runners* (:mod:`adjudication`, :mod:`discovery`) call before they confirm.
63	    A study driving :func:`confirm_whole_corpus`/:func:`confirm_unit_level` directly (outside a
64	    runner) should call ``require_preregistered`` itself to get the real guarantee.
65	    """
66	    if not hypothesis.locked:
67	        raise FirewallViolation(
68	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
69	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
70	        )
71	
72	
73	def _validate_alpha(alpha: float) -> None:
74	    # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
75	    # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
76	    # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.
77	    if not (0.0 < alpha < 0.5):
78	        raise ValueError(f"alpha must be in (0, 0.5); got {alpha}")
79	
80	
81	def _validate_pvalue(p_value: float) -> None:
82	    if not (0.0 <= p_value <= 1.0):
83	        raise ValueError(f"p_value must be in [0, 1]; got {p_value}")
84	
85	
86	def _validate_finite(name: str, value: float) -> None:
87	    if not math.isfinite(value):
88	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a finite number; got {value}")
89	
90	
91	def verdict_from_pvalue(
92	    hypothesis_id: str,
93	    *,
94	    statistic: float,
95	    p_value: float,
96	    alpha: float,
97	    powered: bool,
98	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
99	    test_description: str = "confirmatory test",
100	) -> Verdict:
101	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
102	
103	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
104	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
105	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
106	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here).
107	
108	    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
109	    appropriate to the claim's predicted direction. For a unit-level held-out test the engine
110	    is handed only an opaque ``statistic`` + ``p_value`` and cannot itself infer whether the
111	    statistic supports or contradicts the claim — so ``direction_supports_claim`` is a trusted
112	    caller obligation, ``statistic`` is recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the
113	    verdict), and the deciding flag is recorded in ``evidence`` so the verdict is re-derivable
114	    (audit pass 2, issue #20). Inputs are validated (issue #4) and the recorded decision rule
115	    reflects the caller's ``test_description``, not a hard-coded sidedness label (issue #2).
116	    """
117	    _validate_pvalue(p_value)
118	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
119	    _validate_finite("statistic", statistic)
120	
121	    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
122	    common = dict(
123	        statistic=statistic,
124	        threshold=alpha,
125	        evidence={"p_value": p_value, "direction_supports_claim": direction_supports_claim},
126	    )
127	    if not powered:
128	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
129	    if p_value <= alpha:
130	        if direction_supports_claim:
131	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
132	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
133	    return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common)
134	
135	
136	def confirm_unit_level(
137	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
138	    *,
139	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
140	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
141	    statistic: float,
142	    p_value: float,
143	    alpha: float,
144	    powered: bool,
145	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
146	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
147	) -> Verdict:
148	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only.
149	
150	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
151	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
152	    """
153	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
154	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
155	    # Defense-in-depth direction validation (pass 3, #F-002): the unit-level path takes the
156	    # deciding direction as a trusted caller flag (``direction_supports_claim``) and so never
157	    # routed through ``_resolve_direction`` — which left a locked-but-invalid
158	    # ``predicted_direction`` (e.g. "UP") entirely unchecked here. ``Hypothesis.__post_init__``
159	    # now rejects such values at construction, but re-validate any pre-registered direction here
160	    # so a hypothesis constructed by an unusual path (or a future field mutation) cannot confirm
161	    # against an unrecognized tail.
162	    if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and (
163	        hypothesis.predicted_direction not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS
164	    ):
165	        raise ValueError(
166	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got "
167	            f"{hypothesis.predicted_direction!r}"
168	        )
169	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
170	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
171	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
172	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
173	        statistic=statistic,
174	        p_value=p_value,
175	        alpha=alpha,
176	        powered=powered,
177	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
178	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
179	    )
180	
181	
182	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
183	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
184	    n = len(null)
185	    if tail == "greater":
186	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
187	    else:
188	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
189	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
190	
191	
192	def _resample_stability(
193	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
194	) -> float:
195	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
196	
197	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
198	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
199	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
200	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
201	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
202	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
203	
204	    The default path is O(R·N) pure Python (one null pass per resample). When numpy is available
205	    (the optional ``baseline`` extra) the whole R×N comparison runs as a single vectorized op
206	    (pass 3, #F-017). The numpy path computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts and the same
207	    ``(extreme + 1) / (n + 1) <= alpha`` test, so its result is exactly equal to the Python path;
208	    it is purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change.
209	    """
210	    n = len(null)
211	    try:
212	        import numpy as np
213	
214	        null_arr = np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64)
215	        res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)
216	        if tail == "greater":
217	            extreme = (null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)
218	        else:
219	            extreme = (null_arr[None, :] <= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)
220	        p = (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
221	        return float(int((p <= alpha).sum()) / len(resamples))
222	    except ImportError:
223	        significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
224	        return significant / len(resamples)
225	
226	
227	_VALID_DIRECTIONS = ("greater", "less")
228	
229	
230	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
231	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time
232	    argument is optional and, if given, must match the pre-registered one.
233	
234	    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}`` and any other value is
235	    rejected: an unrecognized direction would otherwise fall through to the 'less' tail in
236	    :func:`_empirical_p`, silently flipping supported↔contradicted (audit pass 2, #128).
237	    """
238	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
239	    if locked_dir is not None:
240	        if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
241	            raise ValueError(
242	                "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
243	                f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
244	            )
245	        resolved = locked_dir
246	    elif predicted_direction is None:
247	        raise ValueError(
248	            "no predicted_direction: set it on the hypothesis at pre-registration "
249	            "(preferred) or pass it explicitly"
250	        )
251	    else:
252	        resolved = predicted_direction
253	    if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
254	        raise ValueError(
255	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
256	            "unrecognized tail would silently flip the supported/contradicted verdict"
257	        )
258	    return resolved
259	
260	
261	def confirm_whole_corpus(
262	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
263	    *,
264	    observed: float,
265	    null_distribution: Sequence[float],
266	    alpha: float,
267	    resample_statistics: Sequence[float] | None = None,
268	    stability_threshold: float = 0.9,
269	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None,
270	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
271	) -> Verdict:
272	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + measured stability.
273	
274	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
275	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
276	
277	    The predicted tail is taken from the hypothesis's pre-registered ``predicted_direction``
278	    (issue #24). The pattern must (a) beat the null at ``alpha`` in the predicted tail and
279	    (b) be **stable across resamples** — measured by the engine from ``resample_statistics``,
280	    not asserted by the caller (audit pass 2, issue #21). Outcomes:
281	
282	    - significant in the predicted tail AND stable → ``supported``;
283	    - significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``;
284	    - unstable, not significant, or stability not assessed → ``indeterminate``.
285	
286	    If ``resample_statistics`` is omitted, stability cannot be measured, so ``supported`` is
287	    unavailable and the verdict is ``indeterminate`` ("stability not assessed").
288	    """
289	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
290	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
291	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
292	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
293	    _validate_finite("observed", observed)
294	    if not null_distribution:
295	        raise ValueError(
296	            "a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation"
297	        )
298	    for v in null_distribution:
299	        _validate_finite("null_distribution value", v)
300	    if not (0.0 < stability_threshold <= 1.0):
301	        raise ValueError(f"stability_threshold must be in (0, 1]; got {stability_threshold}")
302	
303	    direction = _resolve_direction(hypothesis, predicted_direction)
304	    opposite: Direction = "less" if direction == "greater" else "greater"
305	    p_support = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, direction)
306	    p_contra = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, opposite)
307	    n = len(null_distribution)
308	
309	    stability: float | None = None
310	    if resample_statistics is not None:
311	        if not resample_statistics:
312	            raise ValueError("resample_statistics, if given, must be non-empty")
313	        for r in resample_statistics:
314	            _validate_finite("resample statistic", r)
315	        stability = _resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)
316	    stable = stability is not None and stability >= stability_threshold
317	
318	    rule = (
319	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}, predicted tail '{direction}'; "
320	        f"stability across resamples >= {stability_threshold} (engine-measured)"
321	    )
322	    evidence = {
323	        "p_support": p_support,
324	        "p_contradict": p_contra,
325	        "null_n": n,
326	        "predicted_direction": direction,
327	        "stability": stability,
328	        "stability_threshold": stability_threshold,
329	        "n_resamples": len(resample_statistics) if resample_statistics is not None else 0,
330	    }
331	    common = dict(statistic=observed, threshold=alpha, evidence=evidence)
332	
333	    # A contradiction (significant in the opposite tail) is reportable without stability.
334	    if p_contra <= alpha:
335	        return Verdict.contradicted(
336	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="significant in the opposite tail", **common
337	        )
338	    if stability is None:
339	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
340	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
341	            rule,
342	            notes="stability not assessed (no resamples supplied)",
343	            **common,
344	        )
345	    if not stable:
346	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
347	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
348	        )
349	    if p_support <= alpha:
350	        return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
351	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
352	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="does not beat null at alpha", **common
353	    )
354

content.text:
The finding accurately describes the code: `authority` defaults to `None`, and when `None`, only `require_locked` (a presence check) runs. The PoC's claim is that a study can call these primitives directly with a fake `timestamp_proof` and get a verdict.

Let me verify the PoC actually runs and that `require_locked`/`Hypothesis.locked` doesn't itself validate the proof.

content.content:
1	"""Typed, falsifiable hypotheses (METHODOLOGY.md §3).
2	
3	Every hypothesis names what is claimed, the test, the data it runs on, and a pre-specified
4	decision rule. Two origins:
5	
6	- ``DISCOVERY`` — surfaced by the data, then made specific, locked, and timestamped before
7	  confirmation (Firewall B).
8	- ``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` — derived by the adapter from an external expert claim, tested against
9	  the blind baseline (Firewall A).
10	
11	Two kinds, which select the confirmation mechanism:
12	
13	- ``UNIT_LEVEL`` — confirmed on a held-out split.
14	- ``WHOLE_CORPUS`` — no held-out object possible; confirmed against null/permutation
15	  distributions and stability across resamples.
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
21	from enum import Enum
22	from typing import Any, Literal, Mapping
23	
24	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
25	
26	# The tail a claim predicts the statistic lies in, relative to the null.
27	Direction = Literal["greater", "less"]
28	
29	
30	class HypothesisKind(Enum):
31	    UNIT_LEVEL = "unit_level"
32	    WHOLE_CORPUS = "whole_corpus"
33	
34	
35	class HypothesisOrigin(Enum):
36	    DISCOVERY = "discovery"
37	    EXTERNAL_CLAIM = "external_claim"
38	
39	
40	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
41	class Hypothesis:
42	    """A locked, falsifiable assertion ready for confirmatory testing.
43	
44	    ``locked_at`` and ``timestamp_proof`` are populated when the hypothesis is sealed for
45	    pre-registration. An unlocked hypothesis (either field ``None``) must not be passed to a
46	    confirmatory test.
47	
48	    Scope of ``locked`` (audit pass 1, issue #6): ``locked`` is only a cheap **presence**
49	    predicate — both fields populated. It is NOT the methodology-grade check and carries no
50	    cryptographic meaning on its own. The real pre-registration verification —
51	    content-binding (the proof covers this hypothesis's content), timestamp attestation, and
52	    lock-before-confirm ordering — lives in
53	    :mod:`assay_engine.methodology.preregistration` (``verify_preregistration`` /
54	    ``require_preregistered``), and the confirmatory runners (:mod:`adjudication`,
55	    :mod:`discovery`) enforce it via a supplied ``TimestampAuthority``. Use
56	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.lock_hypothesis` to produce a hypothesis
57	    whose lock actually verifies; a hand-set ``timestamp_proof`` string satisfies ``locked``
58	    but will fail ``verify_preregistration``.
59	    """
60	
61	    hypothesis_id: str
62	    statement: str
63	    kind: HypothesisKind
64	    origin: HypothesisOrigin
65	    test_name: str
66	    decision_rule: str
67	    source_claim_id: str | None = None
68	    locked_at: str | None = None
69	    timestamp_proof: str | None = None
70	    # The predicted direction, fixed at pre-registration so it cannot be chosen post-hoc
71	    # after seeing the baseline (audit pass 2, issue #24). For an EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypothesis
72	    # the adapter derives this from the claim's assertion, not from the baseline.
73	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None
74	    params: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
75	
76	    @property
77	    def locked(self) -> bool:
78	        return self.locked_at is not None and self.timestamp_proof is not None
79	
80	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
81	        if self.origin is HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM and not self.source_claim_id:
82	            raise ValueError("EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypotheses must carry a source_claim_id")
83	        # Validate predicted_direction at the earliest possible point (pass 3, #F-002): an
84	        # invalid tail ("up", "UP", "") must never reach lock_hypothesis (which would
85	        # cryptographically bind garbage into the proof) nor confirm_unit_level (which does not
86	        # otherwise re-validate it). Only the two recognized tails — or None (deferred to
87	        # confirm time on the whole-corpus path) — are admissible.
88	        if self.predicted_direction is not None and self.predicted_direction not in (
89	            "greater",
90	            "less",
91	        ):
92	            raise ValueError(
93	                "predicted_direction must be 'greater' or 'less' (or None); got "
94	                f"{self.predicted_direction!r} — an unrecognized tail would silently flip the "
95	                "supported/contradicted verdict"
96	            )
97	        object.__setattr__(self, "params", freeze_mapping(self.params))
98

content.content:
40	    from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact, BaselineBuilder
41	
42	
43	class ClaimConfirmer(Protocol):
44	    """Study-supplied: confirm one claim-derived hypothesis against the (blind) baseline.
45	
46	    The study owns this because only it knows the baseline's structure and how a given claim
47	    maps onto a measurement of it; the engine supplies the confirmatory primitives
48	    (``confirm_whole_corpus`` / ``confirm_unit_level``) the study uses inside it.
49	    """
50	
51	    def __call__(
52	        self, hypothesis: Hypothesis, baseline: BaselineArtifact, claim: ClaimRecord
53	    ) -> Verdict: ...
54	
55	
56	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
57	class SourceScorecard:
58	    """How an external source aligns with the independent baseline (METHODOLOGY.md §5).
59	
60	    ``alignment_rate`` is supported / (supported + contradicted) — the fraction of *decisive*
61	    claims where the blind baseline agrees with the source's assertion. ``indeterminate`` is
62	    excluded from the denominator (the method could not decide; counting it would understate
63	    or overstate alignment). This is a frequency, not a verdict on whether the source is
64	    "good": it says how often an independent reading of the data corroborates the source.
65	    """
66	
67	    source: str
68	    n_supported: int
69	    n_contradicted: int
70	    n_indeterminate: int
71	    alignment_rate: float | None
72	    verdicts: tuple[Verdict, ...] = field(default_factory=tuple)
73	
74	    @property
75	    def total(self) -> int:
76	        return self.n_supported + self.n_contradicted + self.n_indeterminate
77	
78	    @property
79	    def decisive(self) -> int:
80	        return self.n_supported + self.n_contradicted
81	
82	
83	def _score(source: str, verdicts: list[Verdict]) -> SourceScorecard:
84	    counts = {
85	        VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED: 0,
86	        VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED: 0,
87	        VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE: 0,
88	    }
89	    for v in verdicts:
90	        counts[v.label] += 1
91	    decisive = counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED] + counts[VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED]
92	    rate = counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED] / decisive if decisive else None
93	    return SourceScorecard(
94	        source=source,
95	        n_supported=counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED],
96	        n_contradicted=counts[VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED],
97	        n_indeterminate=counts[VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE],
98	        alignment_rate=rate,
99	        verdicts=tuple(verdicts),
100	    )
101	
102	
103	def adjudicate(
104	    corpus: Corpus,
105	    claims_source: ExternalClaimsSource,
106	    *,
107	    baseline_builder: BaselineBuilder,
108	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis],
109	    confirm: ClaimConfirmer,
110	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
111	    source_name: str = "external",
112	) -> tuple[BaselineArtifact, SourceScorecard]:
113	    """Run the full adjudication, enforcing Firewall A by construction.
114	
115	    1. The claims source is kept in this function's local scope and is NOT handed to the
116	       builder; the baseline is built inside a sealed :class:`ClaimBlindGuard` that holds
117	       nothing. This is a **signature-level** guarantee: the builder is never *handed* a
118	       claims source by the engine, so it cannot accidentally consult the claims. It is NOT
119	       frame isolation — a builder that deliberately reflects into this runner's call stack
120	       (``sys._getframe``) could reach ``claims_source``; preventing that would require
121	       running the builder in a separate process, which is out of scope. The guarantee is
122	       against *accidental* circularity, not against a builder that willfully defeats the
123	       firewall.
124	    2. After the baseline exists, each claim is mapped (by ``hypothesis_for``) to its typed,
125	       pre-stated ``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` hypothesis whose ``source_claim_id`` must match the claim
126	       and which must be genuinely **pre-registered** — :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.
127	       preregistration.require_preregistered` checks (via the supplied ``authority``) that the
128	       proof binds this hypothesis's content and that its attested lock time precedes the
129	       **baseline build** (the ordering instant is captured before the build). A claim-derived

230	                f"duplicate hypothesis_id {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} produced for claim "
231	                f"{claim.claim_id!r} — two claims map to one hypothesis, which would "
232	                "double-count it in the scorecard"
233	            )
234	        seen_hypothesis_ids.add(hypothesis.hypothesis_id)
235	        if hypothesis.origin is not HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM:
236	            raise FirewallViolation(
237	                f"claim {claim.claim_id!r} produced a {hypothesis.origin.value!r} hypothesis; "
238	                "adjudicated claims must be EXTERNAL_CLAIM (pre-stated, not data-surfaced)"
239	            )
240	        if hypothesis.source_claim_id != claim.claim_id:
241	            raise FirewallViolation(
242	                f"hypothesis for claim {claim.claim_id!r} carries source_claim_id "
243	                f"{hypothesis.source_claim_id!r} — claim↔hypothesis misattribution"
244	            )
245	        # Pre-registration: the proof must bind THIS hypothesis's content (no post-lock content
246	        # swap) and its attested lock time must precede the baseline build (so the claim-derived
247	        # hypothesis cannot have been tuned to the baseline).
248	        require_preregistered(hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=not_after)
249	        verdict = confirm(hypothesis, baseline, claim)
250	        # A confirmer that forgets to `return` yields None; accessing verdict.hypothesis_id
251	        # would raise an opaque AttributeError instead of a typed firewall error (pass 3,
252	        # #F-021). Guard the contract explicitly.
253	        if not isinstance(verdict, Verdict):
254	            raise FirewallViolation(
255	                f"confirm returned {type(verdict).__name__} for hypothesis "
256	                f"{hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — expected a Verdict"
257	            )
258	        if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:
259	            raise FirewallViolation(
260	                f"verdict for hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} reports hypothesis_id "
261	                f"{verdict.hypothesis_id!r} — hypothesis↔verdict misattribution"
262	            )
263	        verdicts.append(verdict)
264	        if on_step is not None:
265	            # per-claim hook (in order) so a caller can record the pre-registered hypothesis AND
266	            # its verdict to provenance as each happens, not in a post-hoc batch (#93).
267	            on_step(hypothesis, verdict)
268	
269	    return _score(source_name, verdicts)
270

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. The runner calls `require_preregistered(hypothesis, authority, not_after)` at line 248 *before* invoking the study's `confirm` callable at line 249. So in the composed pipeline, the real verification always runs against the same hypothesis object that the study confirmer then passes to `confirm_whole_corpus`/`confirm_unit_level`.

This is exactly what the finding concedes ("defense-in-depth there"). The finding's actual claim is narrower: the *primitives themselves* are unsafe-by-default and a study/test/future-runner driving them directly bypasses real verification.

Let me evaluate whether this is genuinely refutable. The key questions:

1. Does the PoC actually work? — Yes, the code path is exactly as described; a hand-set `timestamp_proof='x'` satisfies `locked`, and with `authority=None` only `require_locked` runs. This is undeniable from the code.

2. Is the concern moot? — The finding explicitly acknowledges the production path is safe (runner pre-verifies). The disputed point is whether "safe-by-default API should require authority" is a real defect vs. a documented design choice.

Let me check whether there's a test that pins this `authority=None` behavior as *intended contract* rather than a bug, and whether the docstrings document it as a deliberate opt-out.

content.text: Let me check the test that pins the `authority=None` behavior and whether the full-verification path is also tested, to understand if this is an intended documented contract.

The test suite for confirm.py only tests `require_locked` blocking *unlocked* hypotheses (line 52) and uses a hand-set `timestamp_proof="rfc3161:deadbeef"` to satisfy the presence gate throughout. There is no test asserting that `authority=None` is a deliberate, safe contract — and notably no test exercises the `authority`-supplied full-verification branch in confirm.py at all.

Now let me make my assessment. As the adversarial refuter, I need to find code/contract/test that makes the PoC impossible or the concern moot. Let me run the PoC to be certain.

content.text:
The PoC runs and produces a verdict (INDETERMINATE in this case, but the PoC's own claim is just "produced a verdict on a hypothesis whose lock was never cryptographically verified" — which is satisfied). The exact label is immaterial; the point is the fake-locked hypothesis was accepted past the pre-registration gate and a Verdict was returned rather than a `FirewallViolation` being raised.

So the PoC is real and reproducible. Now the refuter's question: is the concern moot?

My assessment:
- The production path (`adjudicate` line 248, `discovery` line 147, `pipeline` line 532) always calls `require_preregistered` with a real authority before the study confirmer runs. The finding explicitly concedes this.
- The disputed claim is purely about API safety-by-default for direct/test/future-runner callers. This is a genuine design judgment, not a demonstrable exploit of the shipped product. The behavior is extensively documented as a deliberate opt-out (confirm.py:46 "the caller has then opted out of real verification and owns that choice"; hypothesis.py:48-58 documents `locked` as presence-only with no cryptographic meaning; both primitive docstrings instruct callers to pass `authority` or call `require_preregistered` themselves).

However, the finding is not claiming the production pipeline is broken — it is claiming a "safe-by-default" defect: real verification is opt-in. That is factually true of the code and is a legitimate hardening concern (a future runner that forgets `authority`, or a test/fixture, silently downgrades to a no-op). I cannot point to any code path, contract, or test that *prevents* a direct caller from getting only the presence check. There is no type-level or runtime requirement forcing `authority` to be supplied. The docstrings *document* the opt-out but do not *prevent* the unsafe default.

The standard for refutation is: the PoC is impossible OR the concern is moot. The PoC is demonstrably possible. The concern is mitigated-in-production but not moot — the API contract genuinely makes real verification opt-in, which is exactly what the finding asserts. I cannot disprove it.

input.reasoning:
I could not refute the finding. The code at confirm.py:39-53 is exactly as described: `_gate_preregistration` falls back to `require_locked` (a pure presence check on `locked`, which hypothesis.py:76-78 defines as merely `locked_at is not None and timestamp_proof is not None`) whenever `authority` is None, and `authority` defaults to None on both `confirm_unit_level` (line 146) and `confirm_whole_corpus` (line 270). I reproduced the PoC under the project interpreter (`uv run`): a hypothesis fake-locked via `replace(h, locked_at='2020-01-01...', timestamp_proof='not-a-real-proof')` passes the gate and returns a Verdict (INDETERMINATE for the given numbers) instead of raising FirewallViolation. The exact label is immaterial — the PoC's claim is only that a verdict is produced on a hypothesis whose lock was never cryptographically verified, which holds.

Attempted refutations and why they fail:
1. Production-path safety: The runners do pre-verify. adjudication.py:248, discovery.py:147, and pipeline.py:532 all call `require_preregistered(hypothesis, authority=..., not_after=...)` against a real TimestampAuthority before invoking the study's confirm callable. But the finding explicitly concedes this ("defense-in-depth there"); its claim is about the primitives being unsafe-by-default for direct/test/future-runner callers, not about the shipped composed pipeline being exploitable. So this does not moot the finding.
2. Type/contract enforcement: There is none. `authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None` imposes no requirement; nothing forces a direct caller to supply it. The docstrings (confirm.py:42-46, 151, 275; hypothesis.py:48-58) thoroughly DOCUMENT the opt-out ("the caller has then opted out of real verification and owns that choice") but documentation does not prevent the unsafe default — which is precisely the finding's point ("real verification opt-in" vs "safe-by-default").
3. Test pinning it as intended contract: tests/test_confirm.py only asserts `require_locked` blocks fully-unlocked hypotheses (line 52) and otherwise uses a hand-set `timestamp_proof="rfc3161:deadbeef"` to satisfy the presence gate; no test exercises the authority-supplied full-verification branch, and none establishes the None default as a guaranteed-safe contract.

The finding accurately characterizes a real API-safety defect: a direct/test/future-runner caller of these study-facing primitives (the adjudication docstring at line 48 explicitly tells studies to call them inside their confirmers) silently downgrades pre-registration to a presence-only no-op. No code path, contract, or test makes the PoC impossible or the concern moot.

content.text: Finding M-3 survives my refutation attempt. The PoC is reproducible (verified under `uv run`), and while the production runners pre-verify with a real authority, the finding explicitly scopes itself to the unsafe-by-default API contract of the study-facing primitives — which I could not disprove. Relevant files: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:39-53,146,270`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py:76-78`, with the production safety nets at `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:248`, `discovery.py:147`, and `pipeline.py:532`.
